title: The Coming Clash Between China and Europe
gist: European leaders have abandoned a decade of complacency and now see Chinese industrial overcapacity as an existential threat — a more severe 'China Shock 2.0' than the U.S. faced in the 2000s — pushing the EU toward tariffs and a coordinated, dispersed trade-defense strategy against Beijing.

insights.claim: In 2025, Xi Jinping tried to position China as the responsible great-power alternative to a belligerent United States, inviting the EU to jointly 'uphold multilateralism' and 'oppose unilateralism and bullying,' but Beijing offered no substantive concessions on the unfair trade practices threatening European industry.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: China's diplomatic overtures to Europe are opportunistic messaging, not a basis for a substantive trade deal — treat rapprochement rhetoric skeptically.
insights.quote: uphold multilateralism, safeguard fairness and justice
insights.locator: In 2025, Chinese leader Xi Jinping tried to capitalize

insights.claim: Chinese Premier Li Qiang dismissed European concerns about a 'China Shock 2.0' by telling them to focus instead on 'China Opportunity 2.0,' signaling Beijing's unwillingness to acknowledge the overcapacity problem driving European alarm.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Beijing's dismissive posture increases the odds Europe moves unilaterally to protectionism rather than waiting for a negotiated fix.
insights.locator: Chinese premier Li Qiang recently dismissed the concerns

themes.theme: China Shock 2.0: bigger and more concentrated than the first

insights.claim: A new 'China Shock 2.0' is coming to Europe that will be more severe than the U.S. China shock of the early 2000s, hitting multiple advanced industrial sectors simultaneously — automotive, machinery, chemicals, pharmaceuticals, and green energy — rather than sequentially, and causing immense job losses.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Unlike the first China shock's sequential, sector-by-sector damage to the US, Europe faces concentrated, simultaneous exposure across its industrial core — a bigger and faster shock to price into European industrials.
insights.locator: The new China shock will be different

insights.claim: France proposed establishing a general tariff of around 30 percent on Chinese goods, and Germany's Chancellor Friedrich Merz — despite initial hesitancy — has signaled readiness to act; every EU leader except Spain has tasked the European Commission with developing options for action.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Near-unanimous EU political will (minus Spain) for Commission action makes a formal EU trade-defense package against China increasingly likely.
insights.quote: establishing a level of protection equivalent to a general tariff of around 30 percent vis-à-vis China
insights.locator: Earlier this year, for example, the French government proposed

insights.claim: China's share of global manufacturing rose from 6 percent in 2000 to about 30 percent today, while the EU's share fell from 30 percent to 17 percent over the same period.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: This 24-point manufacturing-share swing is the single clearest metric for the scale of Europe's industrial displacement by China.
insights.locator: China's share of global manufacturing has increased

insights.claim: Since 2015, the value of Chinese exports to the EU has grown 89 percent, quadrupling the trade deficit; China's trade surplus with the EU reached approximately $411 billion last year, and Germany alone faces an expected $114 billion deficit this year.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: The trade-deficit trajectory is accelerating, not stabilizing, adding urgency to EU trade-defense timelines.
insights.locator: Since 2015, the value of Chinese exports to the EU

insights.claim: German industry is losing about 10,000 jobs a month amid the China shock; Herrenknecht founder Martin Herrenknecht said the China shock is real and is hitting German industry with full force.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: A concrete, monthly German job-loss figure gives a trackable leading indicator for the pace of European deindustrialization from Chinese competition.
insights.quote: The China shock is real. And it is hitting German industry with full force.
insights.locator: German industry is losing 10,000 jobs a month

insights.claim: China's competitive edge rests not just on innovation but on subsidies and a currency estimated to be undervalued by 16 to 30 percent; an OECD report found Chinese firms received, on average, three to eight times more government support than firms in OECD countries between 2005 and 2024.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Quantifies the subsidy gap underpinning Chinese export pricing, giving Europe's tariff and countervailing-duty case a hard evidentiary basis.
insights.quote: Chinese firms received, on average, three to eight times more government support than firms in OECD countries
insights.locator: A recent report by the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development

insights.claim: Beijing has built a comprehensive legal and bureaucratic apparatus for export controls, plus intelligence on European supply-chain dependencies, giving it the capacity to weaponize those dependencies to keep European markets open to Chinese goods.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: China's export-control toolkit is a credible retaliation threat Europe must game out before escalating tariffs, not an afterthought.
insights.quote: Beijing has learned to weaponize European dependencies.
insights.locator: At the same time, Beijing has learned to weaponize

themes.theme: The solar precedent and the stakes ahead

insights.claim: The European solar industry fell from 30 percent of global manufacturing share in the early 2010s to just 1 percent today because of Chinese competition; the coming shock could replicate this dynamic at far larger scale, threatening hundreds of thousands of jobs across sectors that would be very costly to rebuild once lost.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Solar's near-total wipeout is the template for what full-scale Chinese competition can do to a European industry within roughly a decade — treat it as the base-rate scenario for exposed sectors.
insights.locator: The story of the German and European solar industry

insights.claim: If Europe's advanced industries are hollowed out, it risks a dependency cycle China could exploit politically, potentially making Europe's rearmament goals impossible since defense-industrial capacity relies on the same threatened manufacturing sectors.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Links industrial-policy failure directly to European rearmament capacity — a channel connecting the China-trade debate to defense-spending credibility.
insights.locator: If these industries are not shielded from Chinese competition

insights.claim: Beijing's actual near-term incentive is to keep the European market open, not close it, since Europe is now the only major high-purchasing-power market still fully open to Chinese goods amid weak Chinese domestic demand — meaning today's cheap pricing could give way to price hikes once local competitors are killed off.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Reframes 'cheap Chinese imports' as a temporary phase of a predatory-pricing strategy, not a permanent consumer benefit — supports the case for early intervention before local competitors disappear.
insights.locator: Today, imports from China may be cheap

insights.claim: Pro-Beijing voices in Europe — including the Global Times, France's Jean-Luc Mélenchon, and Germany's Alice Weidel — are positioned to attack any EU trade-defense measures as reckless and as a cover for European policymakers' own failures.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Domestic political opposition to trade-defense measures is organized and named, a concrete risk factor for the speed and scope of EU action.
insights.locator: Pro-Beijing voices in Europe and Chinese propaganda

insights.claim: A single across-the-board countervailing tariff is the cleanest response but the least politically practical, since member states facing concentrated Chinese lobbying and retaliation threats are unlikely to approve it; analyst Andrew Small's 'art of the swarm' — dispersed safeguards, subsidy investigations, procurement rules, and product-safety audits — is the more viable alternative.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Expect EU action to arrive as a series of incremental, sector-specific measures rather than one sweeping tariff — track the pace of the 'swarm,' not a single headline tariff announcement.
insights.quote: safeguards on surging imports, a wider run of subsidy investigations, procurement instruments deployed across sectors
insights.locator: As a practical alternative, the EU should introduce

insights.claim: Cutting off Chinese access to the European market would act as a major demand shock on China's export industries, worsening its economic woes and accelerating its deflationary spiral, giving Europe real leverage if it maintains unity.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: European market access is a genuine point of leverage over China's own deflationary economy, not just a defensive tool.
insights.locator: Cutting off access to the European market will be a powerful tool

insights.claim: Beijing is expected to try to fracture EU unity by disproportionately retaliating against individual member states, prompting the European Commission to craft a solidarity mechanism to compensate targeted countries and sectors.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: Watch the EU's solidarity-mechanism design as the key determinant of whether member-state unity can survive targeted Chinese retaliation.
insights.locator: Beijing will likely try to play divide and rule

themes.theme: The US angle and the risk of losing

insights.claim: US-EU alignment on China policy is an enormous opportunity, since European manufacturers are the only alternative to Chinese producers in fields like specialty chemicals and polymer intermediates, but Trump has repeatedly acted against this shared interest by targeting European allies with tariffs instead of focusing on Beijing.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: US-EU China coordination remains a live but underused lever; Trump's targeting of European allies is actively squandering it.
insights.locator: The only way for Washington to compete with China's design

insights.claim: Without proper preparation, the EU faces a real risk of losing a trade confrontation with China outright: Beijing could cut off key industrial inputs in retaliation for tariffs, isolating Europe and forcing voters unwilling to bear escalation costs to pressure leaders into capitulation.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner
insights.so_what: A losing scenario for Europe is explicitly on the table if unity and preparation fail — the base case is not automatically a European win.
